title: Getting Started with transitmattr
text: transitmattr lets pull live historical data TransitMatters Data Dashboard API directly R. can get things like: often trains coming (headways) long trains sit stations (dwell times) long takes travel stops (travel times) many people riding (ridership) slow zones affecting service

title: Getting Started with transitmattr
code: # install.packages("pak")  # run this once if you don't have pak pak::pak("transitmatters/transitmattr")

title: Getting Started with transitmattr
code: library(transitmattr) library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union library(ggplot2)

what: Step 1: Check that the API is working
title: Getting Started with transitmattr
text: anything else, ’s good practice confirm API . ’ll get back something like: see "pass" ’re good go.

what: Step 2: Understanding what you get back
title: Getting Started with transitmattr
text: tm_* functions return list — R’s way bundling different pieces data together. Think like backpack: reach grab thing want. helpful trick — str() shows whole structure glance:
code: result <- tm_headways("2024-01-15")  # See what's inside: names(result)  # Grab one piece: result$headways str(result, max.level = 2)

what: Step 3: Pull data for a single day
title: Getting Started with transitmattr
text: functions just need date. can write string "YYYY-MM-DD".
code: # Headways (how often trains arrive) on a specific day headways <- tm_headways("2024-01-15")  # Dwell times (how long trains stop) dwells <- tm_dwells("2024-01-15")  # Travel times between stops travel <- tm_travel_times("2024-01-15")  # Alerts (service disruptions) alerts <- tm_alerts("2024-01-15")

what: Step 4: Pull data over a date range
title: Getting Started with transitmattr
text: analysis requires looking range dates. functions take start_date end_date plus extra options. Available line IDs:
code: # Daily Red Line ridership for January 2024 ridership <- tm_ridership(   start_date = "2024-01-01",   end_date   = "2024-01-31",   line_id    = "line-red" )  # Daily trip metrics for the Red Line trips <- tm_trip_metrics(   start_date = "2024-01-01",   end_date   = "2024-01-31",   agg        = "daily",   line       = "line-red" )

what: Step 5: Turn the result into a data frame
title: Getting Started with transitmattr
text: API gives back list, R analysis tools (like ggplot2) prefer data frame. ’s easiest way convert: Tip: bind_rows() doesn’t work, try .call(rbind, lapply(ridership_raw, .data.frame)) — ’s bit verbose handles tricky list shapes.
code: library(dplyr) library(transitmattr)  # Pull ridership data ridership_raw <- tm_ridership("2024-01-01", "2024-01-31", line_id = "line-red")  # Each item in ridership_raw is one record — bind them into rows ridership_df <- bind_rows(ridership_raw)  # Now it looks like a spreadsheet head(ridership_df)

title: Getting Started with transitmattr
text: data frame can plot ggplot2:
code: library(ggplot2) library(dplyr) library(transitmattr)  ridership_df <- bind_rows(   tm_ridership("2024-01-01", "2024-01-31", line_id = "line-red") )  ggplot(ridership_df, aes(x = as.Date(date), y = count)) +   geom_line(color = "#DA291C") +   # Red Line color   labs(     title = "Red Line Daily Ridership — January 2024",     x     = "Date",     y     = "Riders"   ) +   theme_minimal()

what: Recap: the functions at a glance
title: Getting Started with transitmattr
text: Next, check Red Line Analysis vignette full worked example goes raw API data polished chart.

title: Red Line Analysis: A Worked Example
text: New packages? dplyr cleaning reshaping data; ggplot2 charts. Install install.packages(c("dplyr", "ggplot2")) needed.

title: Red Line Analysis: A Worked Example
text: ridership_raw list item represents one day’s record. Let’s flatten data frame:
code: ridership_raw <- tm_ridership(   start_date = "2024-01-01",   end_date   = "2024-01-31",   line_id    = "line-Red" ) library(dplyr) ridership_df <- bind_rows(ridership_raw) head(ridership_df)

title: Red Line Analysis: A Worked Example
Red Line Analysis: A Worked Example
text: notice: Weekends typically show lower ridership. day unusually low weekday, may service disruption — check tm_alerts() date.
code: library(ggplot2) ggplot(ridership_df, aes(x = as.Date(date), y = count)) +   geom_col(fill = "#DA291C") +   scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +   labs(     title = "Red Line Daily Ridership",     subtitle = "January 2024",     x = NULL,     y = "Riders"   ) +   theme_minimal()

what: 2. Travel time from Park Street to Davis
title: Red Line Analysis: A Worked Example
text: uses aggregate endpoint, gives long-run averages rather individual trips.

previous_headings: 2. Travel time from Park Street to Davis
title: Red Line Analysis: A Worked Example
text: Travel time endpoints need platform stop IDs, parent station IDs. station separate IDs direction (northbound toward Alewife = 0, southbound = 1).

previous_headings: 2. Travel time from Park Street to Davis
title: Red Line Analysis: A Worked Example
code: # Northbound: Park Street (70076) → Davis (70064) travel_raw <- tm_aggregate_travel_times2(   from_stop  = "70076",   to_stop    = "70064",   start_date = "2024-01-01",   end_date   = "2024-01-31" )

previous_headings: 2. Travel time from Park Street to Davis
title: Red Line Analysis: A Worked Example
code: library(dplyr) travel_df <- bind_rows(travel_raw$by_date)  # Travel times come back in seconds — convert to minutes travel_df <- travel_df |>   mutate(     date       = as.Date(service_date),     travel_min = mean / 60   )  head(travel_df)

previous_headings: 2. Travel time from Park Street to Davis
title: Red Line Analysis: A Worked Example
text: Dashed line smooth trend. travel times creep time can signal slow zones accumulating — leads us section 3.
code: library(ggplot2) ggplot(travel_df, aes(x = date, y = travel_min)) +   geom_line(color = "#DA291C") +   geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, linetype = "dashed",               color = "grey40") +   labs(     title    = "Average Travel Time: Park Street → Davis",     subtitle = "January 2024 · Red Line",     x        = NULL,     y        = "Minutes"   ) +   theme_minimal()

what: 3. Slow zones on a specific day
title: Red Line Analysis: A Worked Example
text: Speed restrictions (“slow zones”) places trains must go slower normal, usually track issues. Note: slow_raw$available FALSE, active slow zones day. Try different date check period known track work.
code: slow_raw <- tm_speed_restrictions("line-red", "2024-01-15") library(dplyr) if (!isTRUE(slow_raw$available)) {   message("No slow zone data available for this date.") } else {   slow_df <- bind_rows(slow_raw$zones)   glimpse(slow_df) } library(ggplot2) # Each row is a slow zone segment; visualize by location and speed limit ggplot(slow_df, aes(x = reorder(description, speedMph), y = speedMph, fill = speedMph)) +   geom_col() +   coord_flip() +   scale_fill_gradient(low = "#DA291C", high = "#f9c0c0") +   labs(     title    = "Red Line Slow Zones — January 15, 2024",     subtitle = "Lower mph = more restricted",     x        = NULL,     y        = "Speed limit (mph)"   ) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.position = "none")

title: Red Line Analysis: A Worked Example
text: ’s pattern ’ll use : pull → bind_rows → mutate → ggplot. ’re comfortable , try exploring: tm_aggregate_headways() — often trains come, time tm_line_delays() — delay minutes caused alerts tm_trip_metrics() — -time performance per trip

what: transitmattr — R Client for the TransitMatters API
title: transitmattr — R Client for the TransitMatters API
text: R package TransitMatters Data Dashboard API, providing access MBTA performance metrics: headways, dwell times, travel times, ridership, speed restrictions, .

title: transitmattr — R Client for the TransitMatters API
text: remotes:

title: transitmattr — R Client for the TransitMatters API
transitmattr — R Client for the TransitMatters API
code: # Check API health tm_healthcheck()  # List MBTA facilities tm_facilities()  # Service ridership dashboard summary tm_service_ridership_dashboard()

title: transitmattr — R Client for the TransitMatters API
text: Functions accept either Date object "YYYY-MM-DD" string.
code: tm_headways("2024-01-15") tm_dwells(as.Date("2024-01-15")) tm_travel_times("2024-01-15")  # Dated or current alerts tm_alerts() tm_alerts("2024-01-15")

title: transitmattr — R Client for the TransitMatters API
code: tm_aggregate_travel_times(   from_stop  = "place-pktrm",   to_stop    = "place-davis",   start_date = "2024-01-01",   end_date   = "2024-01-31" )  tm_aggregate_headways(   stop       = "place-davis",   start_date = "2024-01-01",   end_date   = "2024-01-31" )

title: transitmattr — R Client for the TransitMatters API
code: # Ridership for the Red Line tm_ridership("2024-01-01", "2024-01-31", line_id = "line-red")  # Delay summary by line tm_line_delays("2024-01-01", "2024-01-31", line = "line-red")  # Trip metrics tm_trip_metrics("2024-01-01", "2024-01-31", agg = "daily", line = "line-red")  # Scheduled service (optionally filtered to one route) tm_scheduled_service("2024-01-01", "2024-01-31", agg = "daily") tm_scheduled_service("2024-01-01", "2024-01-31", agg = "daily", route_id = "Red")  # Speed restrictions / slow zones tm_speed_restrictions("line-red", "2024-01-15")  # Service hours tm_service_hours("2024-01-01", "2024-01-31", agg = "daily")

title: transitmattr — R Client for the TransitMatters API
transitmattr — R Client for the TransitMatters API

title: Get aggregate dwells — tm_aggregate_dwells

text: ... Named query parameters passed API (e.g., from_stop, to_stop, start_date, end_date). base_url Base URL TransitMatters API. Defaults getOption("tm_dashboard_base_url") production host.
